description: Audited nutrient supplement rules and item-level overrides for values not present in primary data sources (IFCT 2017, INDB). Rules provide safe explicit-zero assignments; item_overrides supply cited positive values.

usda_sr_legacy.name: USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy
usda_sr_legacy.note: Standard Reference Legacy database. Values per 100g edible portion.

usda_fndds.name: USDA Food and Nutrient Database for Dietary Studies (FNDDS)
usda_fndds.note: FNDDS provides nutrient values for foods and beverages as consumed (prepared dishes). Used as proxy for Indian recipe estimation.

domain_b12_plant.name: Nutritional biochemistry — B12 absent in plants
domain_b12_plant.note: Plants lack the enzymatic machinery to synthesize active cobalamin (vitamin B12). Fermented plant foods (idli, dosa, tempeh) may contain B12 analogues produced by bacteria, but these are not reliably active cobalamin and are not counted as B12 sources per WHO/FAO guidelines.

domain_cholesterol_plant.name: Nutritional biochemistry — cholesterol absent in plants
domain_cholesterol_plant.note: Cholesterol is synthesized exclusively by animals. Plant sterols (phytosterols) are structurally distinct and not measured as cholesterol.

domain_caffeine.name: Nutritional biochemistry — caffeine distribution
domain_caffeine.note: Caffeine occurs naturally only in tea (Camellia sinensis), coffee (Coffea spp.), cacao (Theobroma cacao), guarana, yerba mate, and kola nut. Absent from all other plant/animal foods unless added.

domain_alcohol.name: Nutritional biochemistry — ethanol in foods
domain_alcohol.note: Ethanol (alcohol) is absent from whole foods and cooked preparations unless the food is an alcoholic beverage (fermented/distilled) or uses alcohol as a significant ingredient.

domain_added_sugars.name: Nutritional science — added sugars in whole foods
domain_added_sugars.note: Whole unprocessed plant and animal foods contain only intrinsic sugars. Added sugars are zero for these foods when the total sugar content is known (sourced) and the food shows no signs of processing or sweetening.

rules.description: B12 is zero for unfortified plant foods from IFCT vegetarian groups (excluding dairy).
rules.notes: Excludes Ghee (T013) which is animal-derived dairy fat but classified vegetarian in Indian system. Milk and Milk Products excluded from groups_include because dairy contains B12. Fermented foods (e.g. idli batter from Cereals/Legumes) still get zero — fermentation B12 analogues are not reliable active cobalamin.

rules.description: Cholesterol is zero for plant-only foods.
rules.notes: Excludes Edible Oils and Fats group entirely because Ghee (T013) is animal-derived with significant cholesterol (~256 mg/100g). Other oils in that group are plant-derived (zero cholesterol) but handled via separate rule to avoid false assignment to Ghee. Milk and Milk Products excluded because dairy contains cholesterol.

rules.description: Cholesterol is zero for plant-derived oils (Edible Oils and Fats group excluding Ghee).
rules.notes: Ghee (T013) is excluded — it has ~256 mg/100g cholesterol. Vanaspati (T014) is hydrogenated vegetable oil, cholesterol = 0.

rules.description: Caffeine is zero for all foods/recipes except tea, coffee, cocoa/chocolate items.
rules.notes: Uses word-boundary regex to avoid false matches (e.g. 'steak' does not match 'tea', 'gateau' does not match 'tea'). Kehwa/Chai are Indian tea preparations.

rules.description: Alcohol is zero for all seed foods/recipes unless name indicates alcoholic beverage.
rules.notes: Uses word-boundary regex. Toddy (K001 in IFCT) is fermented palm sap with ~4-8% alcohol. Word boundaries prevent false matches like 'drumstick' matching 'rum'.

rules.description: Added sugars are zero ONLY for whole unprocessed plant/animal foods where total sugars are sourced and the food is clearly not sweetened.
rules.notes: Only applies when source 'sugars' value is present (sourced), ensuring we know the total sugar content. Excludes the Sugars group entirely (jaggery, sugarcane juice contain natural sugars that might be considered 'added' in processed contexts). Milk excluded because lactose is intrinsic but INDB recipes with milk may have added sugar. This rule does NOT apply to INDB recipes — recipes may have added sugar and we cannot determine from name alone; those remain 'missing' for later validation.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat = 0.45 µg/100g. Buffalo milk estimated slightly lower based on comparative dairy literature; conservative 0.44 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Buffalo milk B12 not directly in USDA; estimated from cow milk (0.45) with minor adjustment. Indian buffalo milk composition broadly similar to cow milk for B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01077: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D — Vitamin B-12: 0.45 µg/100g.

Paneer is fresh unripened cheese from whole milk. USDA SR Legacy: Cheese, cottage, creamed = 0.63 µg/100g; Cheese, ricotta, whole milk = 0.34 µg/100g. Paneer is more concentrated than cottage cheese. Conservative estimate 0.8 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for Indian paneer. Value estimated from comparable fresh cheeses with concentration factor.

b12.citation: Khoa is milk reduced to ~25-30% of original volume (3-4x concentration). Cow milk B12 = 0.45 µg/100g × ~2.5 concentration factor (accounting for some heat degradation) ≈ 1.1 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct reference for khoa B12. Estimated from milk concentration with conservative heat-loss adjustment. Confidence low due to variability in preparation.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01123: Egg, whole, raw, fresh — Vitamin B-12: 0.89 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#01124: Egg, white, raw, fresh — Vitamin B-12: 0.09 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#01125: Egg, yolk, raw, fresh — Vitamin B-12: 1.95 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#01129: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled — Vitamin B-12: 1.11 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy: Egg white raw = 0.09 µg/100g. Boiling does not significantly alter B12 in egg white. Estimated same as raw.
b12.notes: No separate USDA entry for boiled egg white; assumed equivalent to raw.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Egg yolk raw = 1.95 µg/100g. Hard-boiled whole egg shows slight concentration; yolk B12 estimated same as raw.
b12.notes: No separate USDA entry for boiled egg yolk alone.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01132: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet — Vitamin B-12: 0.76 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Omelet diluted by cooking fat; slightly lower than raw whole egg.

b12.citation: Country hen egg assumed similar to commercial poultry egg for B12 content. USDA SR Legacy: Egg, whole, raw = 0.89 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No separate B12 data for Indian country hen eggs; assumed equivalent to standard poultry eggs.

b12.citation: Country hen egg boiled assumed similar to USDA hard-boiled egg (1.11 µg/100g).
b12.notes: No separate data for country hen boiled eggs.

b12.citation: Country hen omelet assumed similar to USDA omelet (0.76 µg/100g).
b12.notes: No separate data for country hen omelet.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01138: Egg, duck, whole, fresh, raw — Vitamin B-12: 5.40 µg/100g. Boiled value assumed similar.
b12.notes: Duck eggs are notably higher in B12 than chicken eggs.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01138: Egg, duck, whole, fresh, raw — Vitamin B-12: 5.40 µg/100g.
Duck egg omelet estimated from raw duck egg (5.4 µg/100g) with ~15-20% dilution from cooking fat, similar to chicken omelet reduction pattern.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for duck egg omelet.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#01140: Egg, quail, whole, fresh, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.58 µg/100g.
Quail egg boiled assumed same as raw (1.58 µg/100g); boiling does not significantly degrade B12.
b12.notes: No separate USDA entry for boiled quail egg.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#17168: Game meat, goat, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.13 µg/100g.
Goat chops (muscle meat) estimated same as goat shoulder. USDA SR Legacy NDB#17168: Game meat, goat, raw — 1.13 µg/100g.
b12.notes: B12 distribution across goat muscle cuts is relatively uniform.

b12.citation: Goat legs (muscle meat) estimated same as goat raw. USDA SR Legacy NDB#17168: Game meat, goat, raw — 1.13 µg/100g.
Goat brain estimated from beef brain proxy. USDA SR Legacy NDB#13320: Beef, brain, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~9.0 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for goat brain; ruminant brain tissue assumed similar across species.

b12.citation: Goat heart estimated from beef heart proxy. USDA SR Legacy NDB#13321: Beef, heart, raw — Vitamin B-12: 8.55 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for goat heart; ruminant heart tissue assumed similar.

b12.citation: Goat liver estimated from ruminant liver range. USDA SR Legacy: Beef liver raw = 59.3 µg/100g (NDB#13325); Lamb liver raw ≈ 90 µg/100g (NDB#17168). Goat liver conservatively estimated at 60 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Goat and sheep are physiologically similar; goat liver B12 likely in 50-90 µg/100g range. Conservative estimate used.

b12.citation: Goat kidneys estimated from ruminant kidney range. USDA SR Legacy: Beef kidneys raw = 27.5 µg/100g (NDB#13326); Lamb kidneys raw ≈ 52 µg/100g. Conservative estimate 28 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for goat kidneys.

b12.citation: Sheep shoulder muscle meat. USDA SR Legacy NDB#17009: Lamb, shoulder, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~2.3 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Lamb/sheep muscle meat typically 2-3 µg/100g.

b12.citation: Sheep chops estimated same as sheep shoulder. USDA SR Legacy: Lamb raw muscle ≈ 2.3 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#17013: Lamb, leg, whole, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~2.6 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#17168 (lamb variety meats): Lamb, liver, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~86-90 µg/100g. Conservative estimate 86 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Lamb/sheep liver is among the highest B12 food sources.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Lamb, kidneys, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~52 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Lamb kidneys are very high in B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Beef, chuck/shoulder, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~2.6 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Shoulder cuts slightly higher B12 than lean round.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Beef, chuck/round cuts, raw — Vitamin B-12 range: 2.0-3.0 µg/100g. Conservative estimate 2.5 µg/100g for beef chops.
b12.notes: Muscle meat B12 varies by cut; 2.5 is a conservative mid-range estimate.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Beef, round (leg), raw — Vitamin B-12: ~2.4 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Lean beef cuts typically 2-3 µg/100g.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#13320: Beef, variety meats and by-products, brain, raw — Vitamin B-12: 9.0 µg/100g (approximate).
b12.notes: Brain tissue is moderately rich in B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#13321: Beef, variety meats and by-products, heart, raw — Vitamin B-12: 8.55 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#13325: Beef, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw — Vitamin B-12: 59.3 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Liver is the richest natural source of B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#13326: Beef, variety meats and by-products, kidneys, raw — Vitamin B-12: 27.5 µg/100g.
Calf (veal) liver B12 assumed similar to beef liver (59.3 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#17190: Veal, variety meats, liver, raw — Vitamin B-12: 60+ µg/100g range.
b12.notes: Calf liver may be slightly higher than adult beef liver; conservative estimate used.

b12.citation: Calf kidneys B12 assumed similar to beef kidneys (27.5 µg/100g).
b12.notes: No separate USDA entry for veal kidneys specifically.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#10005: Pork, shoulder, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~0.7 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Pork muscle meat has lower B12 than ruminant meat.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Pork, loin chops, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~0.5-0.7 µg/100g. Conservative estimate 0.6 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#10136: Pork, variety meats and by-products, liver, raw — Vitamin B-12: 26.0 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy: Pork, kidneys, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~15 µg/100g (approximate from SR data).
b12.notes: Pork kidneys have less B12 than ruminant kidneys.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#05091: Chicken, broilers or fryers, leg, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: 0.31 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#05096: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: 0.31 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#05062: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, skinless, boneless, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: 0.34 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy: Chicken wing, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~0.28 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Wing meat slightly lower B12 than breast/thigh.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#05027: Chicken, liver, all classes, raw — Vitamin B-12: 16.58 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Chicken liver is a rich B12 source, though less concentrated than ruminant livers.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#05028: Chicken, gizzard, all classes, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.21 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#05140: Duck, domesticated, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: 3.0 µg/100g (approximate).
b12.notes: Duck meat has notably more B12 than chicken.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#05165: Turkey, all classes, leg, meat only, raw — Vitamin B-12: ~1.0 µg/100g.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#15001: Fish, anchovy, European, raw — Vitamin B-12: 3.19 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Indian anchovy species may differ slightly but order of magnitude is similar.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15046: Fish, mackerel, Atlantic, raw — Vitamin B-12: 8.71 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Indian mackerel (Rastrelliger kanagurta) may differ; Atlantic mackerel used as proxy.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15088: Fish, sardine, Atlantic, canned in oil, drained — Vitamin B-12: 8.94 µg/100g. Fresh sardine likely similar range.
b12.notes: Sardines are consistently high in B12 across species. Indian sardine (Sardinella longiceps) expected in similar range.

b12.citation: Catla (Catla catla) is an Indian major carp. Estimated from USDA freshwater carp B12 range (~1.5-3.0 µg/100g). Conservative estimate 2.0 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for catla.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy: Fish, carp (similar freshwater species), raw — Vitamin B-12: ~1.5-3.0 µg/100g. Rohu (Labeo rohita) estimated at 2.0 µg/100g as conservative mid-range.
b12.notes: No direct USDA entry for rohu; carp family used as proxy.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15152: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.11 µg/100g.
Small prawns assumed similar B12 to mixed shrimp species (1.1 µg/100g).
b12.notes: Size variation unlikely to significantly affect B12 per 100g.

b12.citation: Tiger prawns assumed similar to mixed shrimp species (1.1 µg/100g).
USDA SR Legacy NDB#15136: Crustaceans, crab, blue, raw — Vitamin B-12: 11.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Indian crab species may differ; blue crab used as proxy. Crabs are consistently high in B12.

b12.citation: Sea crab estimated same as blue crab. USDA SR Legacy NDB#15136: Crustaceans, crab, blue, raw — 11.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Crab B12 is consistently high across species.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15147: Crustaceans, lobster, northern, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.43 µg/100g.
King size lobster assumed similar B12 to northern lobster (1.43 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15147.
Mud crab estimated from blue crab proxy (11.5 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15136.
b12.notes: Mud crab (Scylla spp.) assumed similar to other crab species for B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15167: Mollusks, oyster, eastern, raw — Vitamin B-12: 16.0 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Oysters are among the richest B12 shellfish.

b12.citation: Tiger prawns (brown) assumed similar to mixed shrimp species (1.1 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15152.
Tiger prawns (orange) assumed similar to mixed shrimp species (1.1 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15152.
USDA SR Legacy NDB#15157: Mollusks, clam, mixed species, raw — Vitamin B-12: 98.89 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Clams are the highest known natural food source of B12. Indian green shell clam assumed in similar range.

b12.citation: White shell clam estimated from mixed clam species (98.9 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15157.
b12.notes: Clam B12 is consistently very high across species.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15166: Mollusks, octopus, common, raw — Vitamin B-12: 20.0 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Octopus and mollusks are exceptionally high in B12.

b12.citation: USDA SR Legacy NDB#15175: Mollusks, squid, mixed species, raw — Vitamin B-12: 1.30 µg/100g.
Hard shell squid assumed similar B12 to mixed squid species (1.3 µg/100g).
Red squid assumed similar B12 to mixed squid species (1.3 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15175.
Small white squid assumed similar B12 to mixed squid species (1.3 µg/100g). USDA SR Legacy NDB#15175.

Ghee is clarified butter. USDA SR Legacy NDB#01001: Butter, salted — Vitamin B-12: 0.17 µg/100g. Ghee is more concentrated (water removed) but B12 may partially degrade during clarification. Conservative estimate 0.6 µg/100g based on concentration factor ~3x with degradation.
b12.notes: Ghee B12 data is sparse. Value is conservative estimate; confidence low.

recipe_overrides._notes: INDB recipes have no ingredient lists, so exact B12 computation is impossible. Overrides are applied only to clearly named categories. Confidence reflects naming-only inference. Values estimated using USDA FNDDS prepared-dish proxies and ingredient-proportion reasoning.

b12.citation: Hot tea (Garam Chai) — INDB shows fat=0.53g, protein=0.39g, calcium=14.2mg per 100g, consistent with ~12% whole milk by weight. Milk B12 ≈ 0.45 µg/100g (USDA SR 01077). Estimated B12 contribution: 0.12 × 0.45 ≈ 0.05 µg/100g prepared beverage.
b12.notes: Conservative lower-bound; actual milk proportion may be higher in Indian masala chai.

b12.citation: Instant coffee with milk — INDB shows fat=0.75g, protein=0.64g, calcium=20.9mg per 100g, consistent with ~17% whole milk by weight. Milk B12 ≈ 0.45 µg/100g (USDA SR 01077). Estimated B12: 0.17 × 0.45 ≈ 0.08 µg/100g prepared.
b12.notes: Indian instant coffee is typically prepared with significant milk (not black).

b12.citation: Espresso coffee (likely latte-style) — INDB shows fat=2.14g, protein=1.75g, calcium=58.1mg per 100g, consistent with ~48% whole milk by weight. Milk B12 ≈ 0.45 µg/100g (USDA SR 01077). Estimated B12: 0.48 × 0.45 ≈ 0.2 µg/100g prepared.
b12.notes: High calcium/fat/protein indicates substantial milk; despite 'espresso' name this is a milk-based coffee preparation.

b12.citation: Afghani chicken — chicken curry dish. Chicken muscle ~0.3 µg/100g raw; assuming ~40% meat by weight in prepared dish with dilution from cream/spices/oil. USDA FNDDS: Chicken curry ≈ 0.1-0.3 µg B12/100g prepared. Conservative estimate 0.15 µg/100g.
b12.notes: No exact FNDDS match for Afghani chicken; estimated from generic chicken curry proxies.

b12.citation: Baked egg — primarily whole egg baked. USDA SR: Egg, whole, cooked ≈ 0.89-1.11 µg/100g. Baked egg with minor additions (butter/seasoning) estimated at 0.8 µg/100g.
b12.notes: High egg proportion expected in baked egg preparation.

b12.citation: Baked fish with cheese sauce — fish (~1-9 µg/100g raw) plus cheese (~1.3 µg/100g). Assuming ~30-40% fish, ~15% cheese sauce in prepared weight. USDA FNDDS: Fish with sauce ≈ 0.5-1.5 µg B12/100g. Conservative estimate 0.7 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Wide range possible depending on fish species used.

b12.citation: Baked stuffed fish — fish as primary ingredient. Assuming ~35% fish by prepared weight with stuffing dilution. Conservative estimate 0.6 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Fish species unknown; mid-range estimate used.

b12.citation: Egg curry (Anda curry) — boiled eggs in curry sauce. USDA: Boiled egg = 1.11 µg/100g; assuming ~40% egg by prepared weight in gravy. Estimated 0.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Typical egg curry has 1-2 boiled eggs in tomato/onion gravy per serving.

b12.citation: Baked eggs in tomato sauce — eggs in tomato sauce (shakshuka-style). Assuming ~35-45% egg by weight. Estimated 0.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Similar preparation to egg curry in terms of egg proportion.

b12.citation: Boiled egg (Ubla anda) — hard-boiled egg. USDA SR Legacy: Egg, hard-boiled = 1.11 µg/100g. Slightly conservative 1.0 µg/100g accounting for possible partial yolk removal or serving variation.
b12.notes: This is essentially a plain boiled egg; high confidence in B12 content.

b12.citation: French omelette/omlet — USDA SR Legacy NDB#01132: Egg omelet = 0.76 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Plain omelet with minimal additions.

b12.citation: Corn omelette/omlet — egg omelet with corn addition. Omelet base ~0.76 µg/100g diluted by corn filling (~30%). Estimated 0.5 µg/100g.
Cheese and mushroom omelette/omlet — egg omelet (0.76 µg/100g) with cheese (~1.3 µg/100g). Combined with mushroom dilution. Estimated 0.6 µg/100g.
Chicken curry — USDA FNDDS: Chicken curry, Indian style ≈ 0.1-0.2 µg B12/100g prepared. Chicken raw ~0.3 µg/100g; ~35-45% meat in curry. Conservative estimate 0.15 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Chicken is low in B12 relative to other meats; curry has significant gravy dilution.

b12.citation: Butter chicken — similar to chicken curry with cream/butter sauce. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Rich sauce dilutes meat proportion; B12 primarily from chicken.

b12.citation: Chilli chicken — stir-fried chicken dish. Similar meat proportion to chicken curry. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
Chicken chowmein — noodle dish with chicken. Significant noodle/vegetable bulk dilutes meat. Estimated 0.12 µg/100g.
Mutton biryani/biriyani — rice dish with goat/lamb meat. Mutton raw ~1.1-2.3 µg/100g; ~25-35% meat in biryani. USDA FNDDS: Lamb pilaf ≈ 0.3-0.6 µg/100g. Conservative estimate 0.4 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Biryani has substantial rice component diluting per-100g B12.

b12.citation: Mutton do piaza — goat/lamb curry with onions. Meat raw ~1.1-2.3 µg/100g; ~40-50% meat in curry. Estimated 0.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Do piaza has higher meat:gravy ratio than typical curries.

b12.citation: Mutton chops — primarily meat with minimal sauce. Goat/lamb muscle ~1.1-2.3 µg/100g raw; bone-in chop ~50-60% meat by served weight. Estimated 0.6 µg/100g.
Keema kofta curry — minced meat (goat/lamb) koftas in curry sauce. Meat proportion ~35-45% in prepared dish. Estimated 0.4 µg/100g.
Keema parantha/paratha — flatbread stuffed with minced meat. Meat proportion ~20-30% of total weight (mainly dough). Estimated 0.25 µg/100g.
Keema pancake — pancake with minced meat filling. Estimated ~25-35% meat by weight. Estimated 0.3 µg/100g.
Fish curry (Machli curry) — fish in curry sauce. Fish raw ~1-9 µg/100g (species-dependent); ~30-40% fish in curry. USDA FNDDS: Fish curry ≈ 0.4-1.5 µg/100g. Conservative mid-estimate 0.6 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Wide range due to unknown fish species. Mid-range estimate used.

b12.citation: Fried fish (Indian style) — battered/fried fish pieces. Higher fish proportion than curry (~50-60% fish by weight). Estimated 0.8 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Fried fish has less dilution than curry preparations.

b12.citation: Fish tikka — grilled/tandoor fish pieces with minimal sauce. High fish proportion (~55-65%). Estimated 0.8 µg/100g.
Prawn curry (with coconut) — prawns raw ~1.1 µg/100g; ~30-40% prawns in coconut curry. Estimated 0.4 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Coconut milk gravy dilutes prawn proportion.

b12.citation: Prawn sandwich — prawns (~1.1 µg/100g) in sandwich with bread/vegetables. Prawn proportion ~20-30%. Estimated 0.3 µg/100g.
Paneer kheer — milk-based dessert with paneer. Milk (~0.45 µg/100g) is ~50-60% of prepared dish; paneer adds ~0.8 µg/100g at ~10-15%. Estimated 0.2 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Kheer is a milk pudding; B12 contribution primarily from dairy.

b12.citation: Apple kheer (Seb ki kheer) — milk-based dessert. Milk ~0.45 µg/100g at ~40-50% of prepared dish with sugar/fruit dilution. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
Carrot kheer (Gajar ki kheer) — milk-based carrot dessert. Similar to apple kheer. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
Moong dal kheer — lentil-based milk pudding. Milk/dairy at ~40-50% with lentil/sugar. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
Cucumber raita (Kheere ka raita) — yogurt-based salad. USDA SR: Yogurt, plain = 0.37 µg/100g; raita is ~55-65% yogurt/curd with vegetables. Estimated 0.2 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Raita is primarily curd/yogurt; B12 from dairy component.

b12.citation: Boondi raita — yogurt with fried gram flour balls. Similar yogurt proportion to cucumber raita. Estimated 0.2 µg/100g.
Mint raita (Pudinay ka raita) — yogurt-based with mint. Estimated 0.2 µg/100g.

Sweetened yogurt (Shrikhand) — strained yogurt dessert. Higher dairy concentration than regular yogurt (~70-80% strained curd + sugar). USDA: Yogurt, Greek style ≈ 0.75 µg/100g; shrikhand estimated lower due to sugar dilution. Estimated 0.3 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Shrikhand is concentrated strained curd; higher B12 than plain raita.

b12.citation: Paneer curry — paneer (~0.8 µg/100g) at ~25-35% in curry gravy. Estimated 0.25 µg/100g.
Chilli paneer — stir-fried paneer dish. Similar paneer proportion to paneer curry. Estimated 0.25 µg/100g.
Kadhai Paneer — paneer in spiced gravy. Similar to paneer curry. Estimated 0.25 µg/100g.
Curd rice (Dahi bhaat) — rice mixed with yogurt. Yogurt/curd ~40-50% with rice. USDA: Yogurt = 0.37 µg/100g. Estimated 0.2 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Common South Indian preparation; primarily rice + curd.

b12.citation: Lassi (salted) — diluted yogurt drink. Yogurt ~40-50% with water/ice. USDA: Yogurt = 0.37 µg/100g. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Lassi is diluted curd; lower B12 concentration than neat yogurt.

b12.citation: Sweet Lassi (Meethi lassi) — sweetened diluted yogurt. Similar to salted lassi with sugar addition. Estimated 0.15 µg/100g.
Bengal fish curry (Bengali machli curry) — fish in mustard/spiced gravy. Bengali fish curries typically use higher fish proportion (~40-50%). Estimated 0.7 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Bengali fish curry tends to be fish-forward.

b12.citation: Kashmiri mutton koftas (Gushtaba) — meat koftas in yogurt sauce. Goat/lamb ~1.1-2.3 µg/100g raw; ~35-40% meat + yogurt contribution. Estimated 0.5 µg/100g.
b12.notes: Gushtaba has both meat and yogurt providing B12.
